title: THE CASE FOR MIXED-ABILITY CLASSES
content_html:
The case for mixed-ability classes

Picture this scene. It’s an English literature lesson in a UK school, and the teacher has just read an extract from Shakespeare’s Romeo and Juliet with a class of 15-year-olds. He’s given some of the students copies of No Fear Shakespeare, a kid-friendly translation of the original. For three students, even these literacy demands are beyond them. Another girl simply can’t focus and he gives her pens and paper to draw with. The teacher can ask the No Fear group to identify the key characters and maybe provide a tentative plot summary. He can ask most of the class about character development, and five of them might be able to support their statements with textual evidence. Now two curious students are wondering whether Shakespeare advocates living a life of moderation or one of passionate engagement.

As a teacher myself, I’d think my lesson would be going rather well if the discussion went as described above. But wouldn’t this kind of class work better if there weren’t such a huge gap between the top and the bottom? If we put all the kids who needed literacy support into one class, and all the students who want to discuss the virtue of moderation into another?

The practice of ‘streaming’, or ‘tracking’, involves separating students into classes depending on their diagnosed levels of attainment. At a macro level, it requires the establishment of academically selective schools for the brightest students, and comprehensive schools for the rest. Within schools, it means selecting students into a ‘stream’ of general ability, or ‘sets’ of subject-specific ability. The practice is intuitively appealing to almost every stakeholder.

I have heard the mixed-ability model attacked by way of analogy: a group hike. The fittest in the group take the lead and set a brisk pace, only to have to stop and wait every 20 minutes. This is frustrating, and their enthusiasm wanes. Meanwhile, the slowest ones are not only embarrassed but physically struggling to keep up. What’s worse, they never get a long enough break. They honestly just want to quit. Hiking, they feel, is not for them.

Mixed-ability classes bore students, frustrate parents and bum out teachers. The brightest ones will never summit Mount

questions.text: The writer describes the Romeo and Juliet lesson in order to demonstrate
options: how few students are interested in literature.
how a teacher handles a range of learning needs.
how unsuitable Shakespeare is for most teenagers.
how weaker students can disrupt their classmates’ learning.
questions.question: The writer describes the Romeo and Juliet lesson in order to demonstrate
questions.explanation:
B — how a teacher handles a range of learning needs

Đoạn đầu mô tả:

some students need simplified texts
some discuss basic plot
stronger students analyse themes deeply

→ writer dùng ví dụ để cho thấy teacher dealing with mixed abilities in one classroom.

→ Đáp án B.

**A type of parrot:**
- diet consists of fern fronds, various parts of a tree and [1]
- nests are created in [2] where eggs are laid.
**Arrival of settlers**
- the [3] of the kākāpō were used to make clothes.

questions.question_text: 27 demonstrate how a teacher handles a range of learning needs.

questions.text: What does the writer say about streaming in the third paragraph?
options: It has a very broad appeal.
It is relatively simple to implement.
It works better in some schools than others.
questions.question: What does the writer say about streaming in the third paragraph?
questions.explanation:
A — It has a very broad appeal

Paragraph 3:
“The practice is intuitively appealing to almost every stakeholder.”

→ almost everyone likes/supports streaming.

→ Đáp án A.
questions.question_text: 28 what does the writer say about streaming in the third paragraph?

questions.text: What idea is suggested by the reference to Mount Qomolangma in the fifth paragraph?
options: students not achieving their full potential
students not being aware of their limitations
questions.question: What idea is suggested by the reference to Mount Qomolangma in the fifth paragraph?
questions.explanation:
C — students not achieving their full potential

Paragraph 5:

brightest students never summit Mount Qomolangma
weaker students don’t enjoy easier walk either

→ both groups fail to reach full potential.

→ Đáp án C.
questions.question_text: 29 idea suggested by the reference to Mount Qomolangma in the fifth paragraph?

questions.text: What does the word ‘scaffolding’ in the sixth paragraph refer to?
options: the factors which prevent a student from learning effectively
the environment where most of a student’s learning takes place
the assistance given to a student in their initial stages of learning
the setting of appropriate learning targets for a student’s aptitude
questions.question: What does the word ‘scaffolding’ in the sixth paragraph refer to?
questions.explanation:
C — the assistance given to a student initially

Paragraph 6:
“provide and then gradually remove this scaffolding”

→ scaffolding = support/help given during learning process.

→ Đáp án C.
questions.question_text: 30 word 'scaffolding' in the sixth paragraph refer to?

questions.text: According to Professor John Hattie of the Melbourne Education Research Institute there is very little indication that streaming leads to ______
questions.question: According to Professor John Hattie of the Melbourne Education Research Institute there is very little indication that streaming leads to ______
questions.groupHtml:
Questions 31–35

Complete the summary using the list of phrases, A–I, below.
questions.explanation:
higher achievements

Đoạn:
“tracking has minimal effects on learning outcomes”

→ very little evidence streaming improves achievement.

→ higher achievements fits summary best.

→ Đáp án: higher achievements

_blockConfig.noteContent: According to Professor John Hattie of the Melbourne Education Research Institute there is very little indication that streaming leads to [31]. He points out that, in schools which use streaming, the most significant impact is on those students placed in the [32], especially where a large proportion of them have [33]. Meanwhile, for the [34], there appears to be only minimal advantage. A further issue is that teachers tend to have [35] of students in streamed groups.

questions.questionHtml:
Is streaming effective?

According to Professor John Hattie of the Melbourne Education Research Institute there is very little indication that streaming leads to 31_______. He points out that, in schools which use streaming, the most significant impact is on those students placed in the 32_______, especially where a large proportion of them have 33_______. Meanwhile, for the 34_______, there appears to be only minimal advantage. A further issue is that teachers tend to have 35_______ of students in streamed groups.

questions.text: He points out that, in schools which use streaming, the most significant impact is on those students placed in the ______
questions.question: He points out that, in schools which use streaming, the most significant impact is on those students placed in the ______
questions.explanation:
bottom sets

Đoạn:
“negatively affect those students assigned to the lowest sets”

→ lowest sets = bottom sets.

→ Đáp án: bottom sets.

questions.text: especially where a large proportion of them have_______
questions.question: especially where a large proportion of them have_______
questions.explanation:
disadvantaged backgrounds

Đoạn:
“higher representation of low socioeconomic class”

→ low socioeconomic class = disadvantaged backgrounds.

→ Đáp án: disadvantaged backgrounds.

questions.text: Meanwhile, for the ______, there appears to be only minimal advantage
questions.question: Meanwhile, for the ______, there appears to be only minimal advantage
questions.explanation:
brightest pupils

Đoạn:
“small benefit for those lucky clever students”

→ clever students = brightest pupils.

→ Đáp án: brightest pupils.

questions.text: A further issue is that teachers tend to have ______of students in streamed groups.
questions.question: A further issue is that teachers tend to have ______of students in streamed groups.
questions.explanation:
lower expectations

Đoạn:
“Streaming ... limits what the teacher feels the student is capable of.”

→ teachers expect less from streamed students.

→ Đáp án: lower expectations.

questions.text: The Vygotsky model of education supports the concept of a mixed-ability class.
questions.question: The Vygotsky model of education supports the concept of a mixed-ability class.
questions.groupHtml:
Questions 36–40

Do the following statements agree with the claims of the writer in the Reading Passage?

In boxes 36–40 on your answer sheet, write
YES  if the statement agrees with the claims of the writer
NO  if the statement contradicts the claims of the writer
NOT GIVEN  if it is impossible to say what the writer thinks about this
questions.explanation:
Writer says:
“If we accept this model, it follows that streaming students ... would be efficient”

→ Vygotsky model actually supports streaming, not mixed-ability classes.

→ NO.

questions.text: Some teachers are uncertain about allowing students to take on MKO roles.
questions.question: Some teachers are uncertain about allowing students to take on MKO roles.
questions.explanation:
Bài nói:
“value of knowledgeable student peers must not go unrecognised”

→ writer supports peer MKO roles.

Không có thông tin teachers uncertain about it.

→ NOT GIVEN.
questions.question_text: 37 Some teachers are uncertain about allowing students to take on MKO roles.

questions.text: It can be rewarding to teach knowledge which you have only recently acquired.
questions.question: It can be rewarding to teach knowledge which you have only recently acquired.
questions.explanation:
Đoạn:
“There is also something exciting about passing on skills and knowledge that you yourself have just mastered”

→ teaching recently learned knowledge is rewarding/exciting.

→ YES.
questions.question_text: 38 It can be rewarding to teach knowledge which you have only recently acquired.

questions.text: The priority should be to ensure that the highest-achieving students attain their goals.
questions.question: The priority should be to ensure that the highest-achieving students attain their goals.
questions.explanation:
Writer concludes:
“we need the many to flourish – not suffer at the expense of a few bright stars.”

→ writer does NOT think priority should only be top students.

→ NO.
questions.question_text: 39 priority should be to ensure that highest-achieving students attain goals.

questions.text: Collaborative outdoor activities improve student outcomes in the classroom.
questions.question: Collaborative outdoor activities improve student outcomes in the classroom.
questions.explanation:
Writer mentions class hikes as analogy/example of cooperation.

But no direct evidence that outdoor activities improve classroom academic outcomes.

→ NOT GIVEN.
questions.question_text: 40 Collaborative outdoor activities improve student outcomes in the classroom.
